summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where the parties have reached agreement on the majority of issues – Where discrete issues remain in dispute – Where the Court considered the child’s right to enjoy their culture pursuant to the s 60CC(3) considerations – Where the Court made orders providing for the child to spend time with a parent on days which celebrate Māori and Aboriginal culture – Where the Court found that a party’s history of acting protectively operated as a safeguard against future risk – Where the child has been primarily cared for by a grandparent since birth – Where serious allegations of drug misuse are made against a parent – Where the parties have taken steps towards rehabilitation and improving parenting capacity – Where the Court declined to make orders restraining a parent from residing at a particular residence.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

McCLELLAND DCJ

INTRODUCTION

These proceedings concern parenting arrangements for X, born in 2017 (“the child”). The child was eight years old at the time of the final hearing and is the sole child of the relationship between the applicant mother (“the mother”) and second respondent father (“the father”). The remaining parties to these proceedings are the first respondent, being the child’s maternal grandmother (“the maternal grandmother”), and the Independent Children’s Lawyer.
Prior to the commencement of the trial, the parties engaged in productive discussions and, as a result, reached broad agreement on most matters relating to the child. This agreement is reflected in the comprehensive consent orders preceding this judgment. Notwithstanding this, certain discrete issues remain unresolved in circumstances where the maternal grandmother has been the child’s primary carer since her birth, with the mother and father maintaining varying degrees of involvement throughout the child’s life. Both parents have, however, demonstrated a genuine interest in the child’s welfare and a desire to strengthen their respective relationships with her moving forward.
The history of this parenting dispute, and the subsequent court proceedings, have been afflicted by several significant challenges; including entrenched parental conflict, substance misuse, and allegations of family violence. To their great credit, both parents have, in recent years, demonstrated meaningful efforts towards rehabilitation and a commitment to improving their parenting capacity. This judgment acknowledges those efforts while also ensuring that the best interests of the child are promoted in all scenarios proposed by the parties.

BACKGROUND

In circumstances where the parties have substantially agreed upon parenting arrangements for the child, and where it is accepted that both parents have made considerable efforts to turn their lives around, I have deliberately refrained from setting out, in detail, the more contentious allegations made by each party against the other. Nonetheless, while the Court is tasked with adjudicating a relatively narrow range of issues, it remains necessary to provide a broad outline of the circumstances which have given rise to the present proceedings and the parties’ respective proposed orders.
By way of summary, the mother and father commenced their relationship in around 2012-2013 and began intermittent cohabitation shortly thereafter. Despite difficulties, the parties reconciled on several occasions following periods of separation before ending the relationship on a final basis in late 2017 after an alleged incident of family violence.
Notably, during a night out in April 2015, the mother was charged with common assault after intervening in an altercation with a third party. She was initially convicted of the offence but successfully appealed this conviction in May 2016. The father was present during the altercation and was also charged as a result of his involvement. During this period, the maternal grandparents provided financial and emotional support to the mother; including by way of payment of legal fees.
In 2016, following a brief separation, the mother discovered she was pregnant with the child. The parties agreed to reunite and resided together at times; firstly in the paternal grandmother’s home and later in a rented property in Suburb J. The mother continued full-time employment throughout her pregnancy despite experiencing illness. In 2016, before the child’s birth, the mother returned to the maternal grandmother’s home due to ongoing conflict with the father. Despite that conflict, the mother maintained contact with the father who, at times, stayed with the her and attended prenatal classes.
The child was born in 2017 with both the father and maternal grandmother present at the hospital on this occasion. Disputes arose almost immediately after the child’s birth. The mother requested early discharge and returned to the maternal grandmother’s home in 2017 following escalating tension between herself and the father. The father stayed briefly in the maternal home before leaving within weeks of the child’s birth. Around this time, the father pressed for the mother and child to relocate to the Suburb J property. The mother, who reports having struggled with breastfeeding and exhaustion, resisted this proposal; citing physical and emotional recovery needs. Communication between the father and maternal grandmother also became strained, culminating in the father’s decision to move out of the maternal grandmother’s home soon after the child’s birth.
In early 2017, the father was involved in an incident in which he was found to have verbally abused police and attempted to seize a firearm. This conduct resulted in the father’s arrest and subsequent sentencing to a Intensive Correction Order in early 2017.
In the months following the child’s birth, the mother and child continued to reside with the maternal grandmother. Initially, the mother facilitated visits between the child and the father at his apartment.
By late 2017, the relationship had deteriorated significantly. The mother reports a particular incident in which the father allegedly became enraged while holding the child, knocking over furniture and shouting. The mother reports leaving immediately with the child and later making a formal police report. Around the same time, the father was sentenced on unrelated charges of resisting arrest and affray.
Late 2017 and early 2018 were marked by mounting concerns regarding the mother’s drug use. It is acknowledged that the mother had a history of illicit drug use, although she abstained during her pregnancy with the child. According to the mother, she resumed drug-taking in 2017 when the child was an infant. By March 2018, evidence of the mother’s relapse was discovered by the maternal grandmother and the maternal grandmother’s husband (“the maternal step-grandfather”), including drug paraphernalia and illicit substance residue. Several incidents followed in which the mother was found to be unresponsive to the child’s needs despite the child audibly crying and the mother being present in the same room. In April 2018, the maternal grandmother reported these concerns to Family and Community Service (“FACS”). Days later, the mother removed the child from the maternal grandmother’s home without notice. Authorities later located the child in May 2018 at a residence with the mother’s new partner, who was, himself, on parole and had a prior firearms charge.
On 2 May 2018, the maternal grandmother filed an Initiating Application seeking recovery orders. On 3 May 2018, the Court ordered that the child be returned to the grandmother’s care and that the mother spend time with the child. Further, interim orders were made allocating parental responsibility jointly to the mother and grandmother.
Pursuant to subsequent orders made 20 June 2028, he father’s contact with the child initially occurred weekly but concerns were later raised by the maternal grandmother regarding non-compliance with supervision conditions. The maternal grandmother also documented concerns regarding the nature of care provided by the father. It has, however, been unnecessary to detail those concerns.
In August 2019, interim consent orders were made confirming the child’s residence with the maternal grandmother and implementing structured contact between the child and both parents. Around this time, the child commenced overnight contact with the father following his production of a clean drug test result. In September 2019, subsequent to the making of those consent orders, the mother entered inpatient rehabilitation for a period of three weeks. Thereafter, the mother commenced, and later completed, a weekly outpatient programme for a further 12 weeks. During this period, the mother returned to the maternal home under strict conditions, including curfews and random drug testing. The maternal family also supported the mother’s recovery by funding therapy and medical costs.
The child commenced spending overnight time with the father in January 2020. The maternal grandmother reports that the child experienced anxiety following stays with the father, including clinginess and distress at bedtime. Concerns were also expressed by the maternal grandmother regarding the father’s unilateral changes to scheduled visits and changeover times, the delegation of the child’s care to paternal relatives during his work hours, and neglect of the child’s hygiene and toileting. Further disagreements arose over relatively trivial matters – such as whether the child would attend a daycare Christmas party. Attempts were made by the parties to engage in joint therapy, as recommended by a child psychologist, but this failed to resolve the ongoing disputation.
In March 2020, the maternal grandmother refused to relinquish care of the child to the paternal grandmother at the commencement of the father’s time in circumstances where the father himself was not present. The father subsequently filed a contravention application on 28 April 2020 alleging failure to make the child available for collection without reasonable excuse. The Court later found no breach on the part of the maternal grandmother, noting the father’s absence.
In June 2020, the mother and maternal grandmother observed signs of genital irritation on the child following her return from overnight time with the father. This discovery prompted the maternal grandmother and mother to schedule a medical appointment with the child’s doctor the following morning. The incident was also reported to the Department of Communities and Justice (“the Department”) and an investigation was commenced shortly thereafter. The Department later disclosed that the paternal grandfather was a known child sex offender with substantiated allegations against two young females known to the paternal family.
On 19 June 2020, the matter came before the Court for an Interim Defended Hearing. Consequently, interim orders were made which restricted the child’s contact with the paternal grandfather. In the following month, as required by the interim consent orders, the parties commenced family therapy. The father and mother continued to attend regular family therapy until late 2022 when the engaged therapist retired. During this period, the child’s contact with the father was adjusted in light of the maternal grandmother’s safety concerns.
During 2021, COVID-19 lockdowns exacerbated conflict between the parties. In August, the father withheld the child on one occasion: citing risks associated with the child’s exposure to the COVID 19 virus. Disputes also arose in relation to vaccinations and the father withholding his consent for the child to obtain a passport – which was later obtained by way of judicial adjudication.
During 2022, the mother demonstrated sustained rehabilitation, employment, and active involvement in the child’s care. She resumed school drop-offs, attendance at the child’s medical appointments and maintained sobriety through ongoing counselling and participation in recovery programmes.
On 22 August 2022, orders were made which provided for the child to live with the mother and spend progressively increasing time with the father; with both parents authorised to exercise joint parental responsibility. These orders also provided for the father to apply to the Registrar of Births, Deaths and Marriages to include “[Irving]” to the child’s registered last name by way of hyphenation. Importantly to one issue remaining in dispute, the father has not utilised the authority granted under those orders as at the date of the Final Hearing.
In October 2022, concerns arose regarding the father’s conduct while the child was in his care. The mother and maternal grandmother both report disclosures by the child about an incident in which the father drove at excessive speed, engaged in racing behaviour with another vehicle, and threw coins at the other car. It was further noted that the child was not restrained in a booster seat. The matter was subsequently reported to the police but no further action was taken. Notwithstanding this, records acquired by the Court under s 67ZBE orders indicate that the father was charged with four separate traffic-related offences in 2022.
On 14 October 2022, the mother reports to have discovered handwritten notes belonging to the father in the child’s notebook – the contents of which led the mother to conclude that the father was involved in selling illicit drugs.
In February 2023, the father informed the mother that he and the child had been involved in a motor vehicle accident. Although the child was assessed for whiplash and found to be in good health, the mother was later advised that the child was not seated in a booster seat at the time of the collision. This was despite the mother’s repeated requests that the father ensure the child travelled in one.
In late 2022, the mother confronted the father after observing drug paraphernalia in his home when delivering the child prior to a scheduled visit. This confrontation resulted in an incident of alleged family violence perpetrated by the father which was later reported to police by the mother.
In April 2023, during the Easter school holidays, the father refused to return the child in non-compliance with Court orders; alleging the child was fearful of returning home due to purported conflict within the maternal household and accusing the mother of illicit drug use. It was also reported that the father intended to retain the child until an investigation by the Department of Communities and Justice had been completed. The mother sought urgent legal intervention and requested that police conduct a welfare check on the child. The police later confirmed that the child was safe in the care of the paternal grandmother and was returned after approximately twelve days.
In late 2023, the mother was the victim of a sexual assault, which she reports precipitated a relapse into drug use. In January 2024, interim orders were made restricting the mother’s time with the child to supervised visits at a contact centre – typically once a fortnight for ninety minutes –as well as occasional Zoom calls. This arrangement was scheduled to continue until December 2024 but the Contact Centre advised that it could no longer facilitate the visits.
In early 2024, the mother altered a hair follicle test report to conceal illicit substance use and was subsequently charged with drug possession which resulted in her being subject to strict bail conditions. Later, in early 2024, a very close friend of the mother was killed in an accident. The mother described this event as enormously distressing and stated that it contributed significantly to her emotional decline during that period. This loss, combined with the earlier sexual assault in late 2023, left the mother in a very dark place and is recognised as being a key factor in her relapse into drug use.
Subsequent to events giving rise to charges of drug possession, police conducted a bail check at the maternal grandmother’s residence in the early hours of the morning, causing some disruption to the maternal grandmother’s household and contributing to the eviction of the mother from the residence in July 2024.
In late 2024, the mother was arrested and charged with a weapons offence and additional charges of possession of illicit drugs. These criminal proceedings were finalised in late 2025; with the mother being placed on a Community Corrections Order for a period of twelve months.
In 2025, the mother’s time with the child fluctuated greatly. However, she resumed residing at the maternal grandmother’s home at this time – albeit frequently spending nights away.
While the mother has had an unfortunate history of illicit drug use, as of mid-late 2025, she has demonstrated a renewed and sustained commitment to overcoming addiction. By November 2025, she had achieved a period of thirty consecutive days free from illegal substances – only utilising prescribed medicinal cannabis – and has expressed a clear determination to maintain her sobriety. She has also actively engaged with her local Narcotics Anonymous group – drawing support from peers and a structured recovery network – and indicated her intention to participate in the Family Connect and Support Programme offered by the Department of Communities and Justice to strengthen her rehabilitation and family relationships.
The mother has also acknowledged the gravity of her prior conduct and shown insight into the circumstances of her relapse. Furthermore, she has conveyed a strong motivation to rebuild her relationship with the child in a manner consistent with her recovery goals.
The father’s time with the child was curtailed following the April 2023 incident in which the father withheld the child. However, time between the father and the child has gradually been restored; first under supervision before later progressing to unsupervised time. It is accepted that this progression has occurred in circumstances where he father has demonstrated a recognition of the gravity of his past conduct, including criminal conduct, and has undertaken steps towards rehabilitation.
The father has acknowledged that his involvement in violent offending – including the 2015 incident in City K which resulted in a conviction for assault occasioning actual bodily harm – stemmed from poor impulse control and inadequate anger management. In response, the father has engaged with a Drug and Alcohol counsellor at L Health Services and completed an anger management programme, together with the “Keeping Kids in Mind” parenting course. As of November 2025, he reports to remain engaged with a psychologist at M Mental Health Services. The father has also complied with the requirements of an Intensive Correction Order and a subsequent good behaviour bond, without incurring any further violent offences during the supervisory period. In addition, the father has expressed insight into the detrimental impact of his prior behaviour on his parenting capacity and has taken steps to improve emotional regulation through continued psychological support and structured interventions; thereby demonstrating a commitment to addressing the underlying issues that contributed to his past offending.
From January 2024, interim orders provided for the father to spend no less than two hours each fortnight with the child under supervision at C Contact Centre. On 20 December 2024, further orders were made removing the requirement of supervision with time commencing each alternate Sunday from 10.00am until 1.00pm, together with video calls (Zoom) each Thursday between 4:30pm and 5:30pm. This time would increase over a six month period; culminating in unsupervised time on both the first, second, and third Sunday of each month from 10.00am until 5.00pm. Provision was also made for occasional adjustments for special events, such as birthdays and family gatherings –including the child’s sibling’s birthday – with scope for some weekends to be substituted or extended by agreement.
Lastly, it is accepted that, for most of the child’s life, the primary responsibility for the child’s daily care, routine, and emotional support fell to the maternal grandmother and step-grandfather.

CONSENT ORDERS

To their great credit, the parties, assisted by their legal advisers, have reached agreement on the most significant aspects of parenting arrangements for the child; culminating in consent orders for the child to live with the maternal grandmother and that she exercise sole parental responsibility. In doing so, the parties have demonstrated a child-focused approach to the child’s welfare and an acknowledgment that the child “shares close affection and familiarity with each of them” (Family Report dated 18 June 2025 at paragraph [85]).
The minute of partial consent orders –jointly tendered at the Final Hearing and amended orally by the parties – is set out immediately below. For the purposes of this judgment, the underlined text reflects the narrow issues remaining in dispute. Furthermore, where the father and maternal grandmother have proposed competing arrangements, their respectively proposed orders have been included and differentiated by use of the “/” symbol.
CREHAN, HINGAIA AND BERGIN

PAC 1889/2018

Proposed Minute of Order

Prior parenting orders:
All prior parenting orders are discharged

Live with arrangements:
The child, [X] born [2017] shall live with the maternal grandmother, [Mrs Eeli].

Decision making:
The maternal grandmother shall have sole decision making authority for any health or education decision in relation to [X] subject to the following:
The maternal grandmother shall consult with each of the parents in writing no less than 14 days prior to any health or education decision, unless in an emergency;
The parents shall provide a written response to the maternal grandmother within 7 days of receiving notification from her of her proposed decision and in the event that such written response is not provided within 7 days, the maternal grandmother shall be entitled to assume that the parent does not wish to be involved in the decision;
The maternal grandmother shall consider any written response provided by either of the parents providing their input into the decision to be made for [X];
The maternal grandmother shall inform each of the parents in writing of the decision ultimately made by her within 48 hours of such decision being made.
The father shall have sole decision making authority for any decision in relation to [X’s] Aboriginal and Māori culture and shall notify the mother and the maternal grandmother of any decision made by him in relation to [X’s] Aboriginal or Māori culture within 48 hours of such decision being made.
The Maternal Grandmother is at liberty to attend such cultural events with [X] that [X] should wish to attend at times when [X] is ordinarily in the maternal grandmother’s care, and the Maternal Grandmother will inform the father of such no less than 48 hours prior to the event, prior to the event taking place.
The father shall continue [X’s] involvement in the “Koori Kids” programme at her school.

Mother’s time and communication with [X]:
Until 31 December 2025, [X] shall spend supervised time with the mother for no less than 2 hours each fortnight, such time to be facilitated by [C Contact Centre].
The mother shall be at liberty to attend Christmas Day celebrations with the maternal family in 2025, subject to the maternal grandmother or her nominee supervising the mother’s time with [X] during this period and the mother shall not stay overnight in the maternal grandmother’s home and provided that the mother undertakes a chain of custody urinalysis test 48 hours prior to Christmas Day which returns a clean result with the father to solely bear the costs of the testing.
For the purposes of Order 8 above, the mother shall authorise the testing laboratory which conducts the urinalysis to provide the results of that testing directly to the father and the mother and these Orders shall constitute authority for the father and the maternal grandmother to request those results directly from the testing laboratory.
From 10 January 2026, [X] shall spend supervised time with the mother for no less than 2 hours on one occasion each alternate weekend, such time to be facilitated by [B Contact Centre] in [Suburb D] (or such other professional supervision service agreed between the maternal grandmother and the mother in writing) as can be accommodated by them.
Subject to the mother’s compliance with Orders 23-25 below, and the mother providing one hair follicle test clear of illicit substances pursuant to Order 23 below, commencing in March 2026, the mother shall spend time with [X] as follows:
From 10am until 2pm on every third Sunday commencing on Sunday 15 March 2026, such time to be supervised by either the maternal grandmother or her nominee;
On Mother’s Day 2026, from 10am until 2pm, such time to be supervised by either the maternal grandmother or her nominee;
On [the child’s birthday], such time to be supervised by either the maternal grandmother or her nominee provided the mother undertakes a chain of custody urinalysis test 48 hours prior to [the child’s birthday] which returns a result clear of illicit substances with the father to solely bear the costs of the testing.
On [the child’s birthday] in 2027 from 2pm until 6pm, such time to be supervised by either the maternal grandmother or her nominee;
Commencing on Saturday 9 January 2027, from 10am until 5pm on every third Saturday and Sunday, such time to take place on an unsupervised basis;
Commencing in 2027, on Mother’s Day each year from 10am until 5pm;
Commencing in 2028, on [the child’s birthday] each year from 2pm until 6pm;
Such other times as may be agreed between the mother and the maternal grandmother in writing.
In the event that the mother’s drug testing results referred to in Orders 23-25 below for any test that is to occur after 15 March 2026 indicate a positive result for illicit substances, or she does not comply with the testing regime, the mother’s time shall remain supervised by the maternal grandmother or her nominee as provided for in Order 11(a) above until such time as the mother provides the maternal grandmother and father with four consecutive hair follicle tests which do not detect any illicit substances, such tests to be undertaken no more frequently than once every three months.
The mother’s time referred to in Order 11 above shall remain supervised until such time as the mother is able to provide the maternal grandmother and the father with four consecutive hair drug tests which do not detect any illicit substances.
To facilitate Order 10 above, the parties shall:
Within 3 days of the making of these Orders, make contact with [B Contact Centre] or such other professional supervision service as agreed between the maternal grandmother and the mother in writing (“the Centre”) and arrange to complete any intake assessment required by the Centre;
Attend all appointments with the Centre as directed by the Centre staff;
Make [X] available for appointments with the Centre as directed by the Centre including any orientation appointments.
The mother shall be solely responsible for the fees for the [C and B Contact Centres].
The mother shall have video communication with [X] as agreed in writing between the mother and the maternal grandmother but failing such agreement, each Tuesday between 4.30pm and 5.30pm with the maternal grandmother to facilitate this communication by forwarding a zoom link to the mother’s email of [...].

Father’s time with [X]:
[X] shall spend time with the father as agreed between the father and the maternal grandmother in writing but failing such agreement as follows:
On 7, 14 and 21 December 2025 from 8.30am until 5pm / from 8.30am until 3.30pm;
Commencing Saturday 3 January 2026, every third weekend from 10am / 11am Saturday until 5pm Sunday;
Commencing Friday 17 April 2026, every third weekend from after school Friday (or 3pm if a non-school day) until 5pm Sunday;
Commencing Friday 10 July 2026, every third weekend from after school Friday (or 3pm if a non-school day) until before school Monday (or 9am if a non-school day) / from after school Thursday (or 3pm if a non-school day) until 5pm Sunday;
Commencing Thursday 1 October 2026, every third weekend from after school Thursday (or 3pm if a non-school day) until before school Monday (or 9am if a non-school day);
Such other times as may be agreed between the father and the maternal grandmother in writing.

School holidays
Commencing in the 2026 Term 3 school holiday period and each school holiday period thereafter, any time that the father spends with [X] pursuant to Order 17(e) above shall be extended to include a 5th night with the father. In the event the father’s time falls on any weekend other than the final weekend of the school holiday period, the father’s 5 nights shall occur from 3pm Wednesday until 5pm Monday . In the event that the father’s time falls on the final weekend of the school holiday period, the father’s 5 nights shall occur from 3pm Tuesday until 5pm Sunday.

Special occasions
Notwithstanding any other order, the father shall spend time with [X] on the following special occasions:
On [the child’s birthday] in 2026 / each year, from 10am until 2pm / from 2pm until 6pm;
On Father’s Day each year from 5pm on the Saturday before Father’s Day until before school the following Monday / from 10am until 5pm;
Commencing in 2025 and each alternate year thereafter, from 3pm on 25 December until 5pm on 26 December / from 5pm 24 December until 11am 25 December;
Commencing in 2026 and each alternate year, from 1pm on 24 December until 3pm on 25 December / from 11am on 25 December until 11am on 26 December;
Commencing in 2026 and each alternate year thereafter, from 1pm on [the day prior] until 1pm on [the child’s birthday] unless otherwise agreed between the parties in writing;
Commencing in 2028 and each alternate year thereafter, from 10am until 2pm on [the child’s birthday];
Waitangi Day – two weeks prior to Waitangi Day (6 February), the father will nominate to the maternal grandmother an event the father wishes to attend with [X] and the maternal grandmother shall make [X] available to attend that event with the father, subject to the father not removing [X] from school for the purposes of this event.
NAIDOC Week – two weeks prior to NAIDOC week, the father will nominate to the maternal grandmother an event the father wishes to attend with [X] and the maternal grandmother shall make [X] available to attend that event with the father.
On 26 May each year from 9am / 10am until 5pm for the purposes of taking [X] to a Sorry Day event, subject to the father not removing [X] from school for the purposes of this event unless it is a sanctioned and/or organised school event that is taking place away from the school grounds;
On 26 January each year from 9am / 11am until 5pm;
Such other times as may be agreed between the maternal grandmother and the father in writing.
For the purposes of changeover and unless otherwise agreed between the father and maternal grandmother in writing, the father shall collect [X] from school (if a school day) or from [E Business] [Suburb F] (if a non- school day) at the commencement of his time and the father shall return [X] to her school (if a school day) or [E Business] [Suburb F] (if a non-school day) at the conclusion of his time.
The father shall have video communication with [X] as agreed in writing between the father and the maternal grandmother but failing such agreement, each Thursday between 4.30pm and 5.30pm with the father to facilitate this communication by forwarding a zoom link to the maternal grandmother’s email of [...].
The maternal grandmother shall ensure that [X] is afforded privacy during her zoom calls with the father.

Drug testing:
The mother shall, at her own cost and on a 3 monthly basis submit to chain of custody hair testing for illicit drugs and to give effect to this Order:
The mother shall submit to chain of custody testing on no less than 4 occasions with the first occasion to occur on or about 27 February 2026;
The mother shall not provide a further hair sample for testing until such time as 3 months has passed from the previous test.
The testing is to be conducted by an approved laboratory, accredited to conduct hair drug and alcohol testing to the recognised international standard ISO/IEC 17024:2005 by the relevant national accreditation body for that laboratory and in compliance with the International Society of Hair Testing (SoHT) guidelines;
The mother shall maintain a head hair length of no less than 5cm;
The mother shall refrain from colouring or bleaching her hair;
The mother shall authorise and direct the testing service to screen the hair sample provided by her for drugs of abuse including amphetamine type substances and their metabolites, cannabis and its metabolites and opioids and their metabolites.
Upon the Mother’s compliance with Order 23, and for a further 12 months, the Mother shall submit to a chain of custody hair testing for illicit drugs within seven (7) days of any request from either the Father or the Maternal Grandmother and to give effect to this Order:
The testing is to be conducted by an approved laboratory, accredited to conduct hair drug and alcohol testing to the recognised international standard ISO/IEC 17024:2005 by the relevant national accreditation body for that laboratory and in compliance with the International Society of Hair Testing (SoHT) guidelines
The mother shall maintain a head hair length of no less than 5cm;
The mother shall refrain from colouring or bleaching her hair
The mother shall authorise and direct the testing service to screen the hair sample provided by her for drugs of abuse including amphetamine type substances and their metabolites, cannabis and its metabolites and opioids and their metabolites.
The mother shall authorise and direct the testing service to provide a copy of the results of the mother’s hair test results directly to the father and the maternal grandmother.
The Father or Maternal Grandmother shall not make more than one request in any six (6) calendar months.
The payment of the hair testing will be at the requester’s expense.
The mother shall authorise the testing laboratory which conducts the hair testing to provide the results of that testing directly to the father and the maternal grandmother and these Orders shall constitute authority for the father and the maternal grandmother to request those results directly from the testing laboratory.

Restraints:
Each party is restrained and injuncted from:
Exposing or allowing [X] to be exposed to family violence;
Using any form of physical discipline on [X] or allowing any other person to do so;
Allowing [X] to be in presence of a person under the influence of illicit substances;
Consuming alcohol in excess of a 0.05 blood alcohol limit whilst [X] is in their care or during the 24 hours prior to [X] coming into their care;
Denigrating or threatening the other party or any member of the other party’s extended family in the presence or hearing of [X] or allowing any other person to do so;
Discussing the court proceedings in the hearing or presence of [X] or allowing any other person, other than a court appointed expert or the Independent Children’s Lawyer to do so;
Allowing [X] to have any unsupervised contact with the paternal uncle, [Mr G] or the paternal grandfather, [Mr H].
That the Mother is restrained from staying overnight at the Maternal Grandmother’s residence whilst [X] is present until the Mother has produced four consecutive hair drug tests which do not detect any illicit substances pursuant to Order 23 and following the further 12-month period pursuant to Order 24 above.
The Mother is restrained by injunction from residing at the Maternal Grandmother’s home until 2031 when the child is fourteen (14) years old.

Ancillary issues:
The maternal grandmother shall notify the father immediately in the event that she is aware or becomes aware that the mother has consumed illicit drugs or is affected by illicit drugs during any period of time that the mother is in the presence of [X] and shall immediately remove [X] from the mother’s presence.
That the father [Mr Irving] be authorised to apply to the Registrar Births, Deaths and Marriages for the child registered as [X Ila] born [2017] to now be registered as [X Ila-Irving] within 42 days of the date of these Orders.
The parties shall do all things necessary to ensure that [X’s] school is provided with a copy of her current birth certificate and that her enrolment is amended to reflect her name as being “[X Ila-Irving]”.
The parties shall communicate with each other about any issues relating to the parenting or [X] by email or text message except in the case of an emergency when such communication will take place by telephone.
Each party shall keep the other informed of their residential address, email address and mobile telephone and shall notify the other party of any change to these details within 48 hours of such change occurring.
These Orders shall constitute authority for each party to obtain information from [X’s] school as to her educational progress and the maternal grandmother, the mother and the father shall all be named as a contact person on [X’s] school enrolment forms.
These Orders shall constitute authority for each party to attend any school, sporting or extra curricular event involving [X] to which parents or caregivers are ordinarily invited or permitted to attend. The mother shall not be permitted to attend any such school events until such time as she has provided the maternal grandmother and the father with 2 consecutive hair follicle tests that are clear of illicit substances / The mother shall not be permitted to attend any such school events unless the maternal grandmother is also present at the event for the periods during which the mother’s time with [X] is to be supervised in accordance with these orders.
Each party shall notify the other of any serious illness, medical emergency or hospitalisation of [X] as soon as is reasonably practicable and shall provide the other parties with details of the medical treatment and health professional treating [X] such that each party is able to obtain information from [X’s] health professionals as to her treatment.
For the purposes of Section 11 of the Australian Passports Act 2005:
It is noted that these orders allow the child [X] born in [2017] to travel internationally
[X] is permitted to have an Australian passport or travel related document provided the application for that document (or renewal) is made by the maternal grandmother, [Mrs Eeli], who may sign any declaration on the application in the form approved by the relevant Minister and the consent of [X’s] parents is not required
[X’s] passport is to be released and held by the maternal grandmother.
The maternal grandmother shall be at liberty to travel internationally with [X] provided such travel does not include travel to a non-Hague country or a country for which travel warnings of “do not travel” or “reconsider your need to travel” have been issued by the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade, subject to Orders 39 and 40 below.
In the event that the maternal grandmother proposes to travel internationally with [X], she shall provide each of the parents with no less than 8 weeks written notice of her intention and travel shall occur not more than once each calendar year for a period of no more than 28 days.
No less than 3 weeks prior to the intended travel, the maternal grandmother shall provide [X’s] parents with the following:
A copy of [X’s] return ticketing information;
An itinerary for the travel period including details as to the address or addresses where [X] will be staying whilst overseas, a telephone contact number that [X] can be contacted on whilst overseas and details of all adults travelling with [X];
A proposal in relation to the parents’ to have telephone communication or video calls with [X] on no less than one occasion per 7 day period that [X] is overseas;
A proposal for any make up time required as a result of the maternal grandmother travelling during a period when the mother or the father (or both of them) would ordinarily be spending time with [X].
Commencing on 1 January 2027 / 2028, the father shall be at liberty to travel internationally with [X] provided such travel does not include travel to a non-Hague country or a country for which travel warnings of “do not travel” or “reconsider your need to travel” have been issued by the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade, subject to Orders 42 and 43 below.
In the event that the father proposes to travel internationally with [X], he shall provide the maternal grandmother and the mother with no less than 8 weeks written notice of his intention and travel shall occur not more than once each calendar year for a period of no more than 10 / 21 days.
No less than 3 weeks prior to the intended travel, the father shall provide the maternal grandmother and the mother with the following:
A copy of [X’s] return ticketing information;
An itinerary for the travel period including details as to the address or addresses where [X] will be staying whilst overseas, a telephone contact number that [X] can be contacted on whilst overseas and details of all adults travelling with [X];
A proposal in relation to the maternal grandmother and the mother to have telephone communication or video calls with [X] on no less than one occasion per 7 day period that [X] is overseas;
A proposal for any make up time required as a result of the father travelling during a period when the mother and maternal grandmother would ordinarily be spending time with [X].
No less than 7 days prior to any intended travel by the father with [X], the maternal grandmother shall provide the father with [X’s] passport and the father shall return [X’s] passport to the maternal grandmother within 7 days of his travel period ending.
Subject to any waiver, the maternal grandmother, father and mother shall share equally in the costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer in the amount of $26,913.00 with each of them to pay an amount $8,971.00, such costs to be paid within 3 months of the date of these Orders.
The maternal grandmother shall do all things necessary to facilitate [X’s] attendance upon the Independent Children’s Lawyer for the purposes of the Independent Children’s Lawyer explaining the effect of these Orders to [X], after which the Independent Children’s Lawyer shall be discharged.

ISSUES REQUIRING ADJUDICATION

The issues requiring adjudication, as reflected in the underlined sections of the above minute of consent orders, are summarised below:
(1) Should the mother be restrained from attending the maternal grandmother’s home on Christmas Day in 2025 and on the child’s birthday in 2026 without having undergone a drug test within 48 hours?

(2) What should the time arrangements be between the father and the child commencing 3 January 2026, 10 July 2026, and 1 October 2026?

(3) What should the time arrangements be between the father and the child during school holidays?

(4) What should be the precise start and end times for the time between the father and the child on special occasions and cultural events, including Father’s Day and Christmas Day?

(5) Should the father be permitted to remove the child from school for the purpose of attending cultural events?

(6) Should the mother be restrained by injunction from residing at the maternal grandmother’s home until 2031 when the child turns 14?

(7) Should the father be authorised to apply to the Registrar of Births, Deaths and Marriages to change the child’s name to “[X Ila-Irving]”?

(8) Should the mother be prohibited from attending school events until she has provided two consecutive clean test results for illicit substances?

(9) What is the maximum period the father should be permitted to travel with the child internationally each year, from which year such travel shall commence, and should compensatory time be granted to the maternal grandmother?

STATUTORY FRAMEWORK AND LEGAL PRINCIPLES

While most parenting orders have been agreed between the parties, some narrow issues remain in dispute and will require judicial determination. In adjudicating the remaining issues in dispute, I am required to have regard to the best interests of the child. In that respect, s 65AA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (‘the Act”), by reference to s 60CA, provides that:
in deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to a child, a court must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.
Guidance as to those matters which must be considered in determining what orders are in the best interests of the child are set out in s 60CC(2) of the Act. Those considerations are as follows:
General Considerations
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);
(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child's developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs ;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child's parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.
(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child's family.
Additionally, s60CC(3) of the Act provides that, where relevant, the Court must consider:
(a) the child's right to enjoy the child's Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander culture, by having the support, opportunity and encouragement necessary:
(i) to connect with, and maintain their connection with, members of their family and with their community, culture, country and language; and

(ii) to explore the full extent of that culture, consistent with the child's age and developmental level and the child's views; and

(iii) to develop a positive appreciation of that culture; and
(b) the likely impact any proposed parenting order under this Part will have on that right.

CONSIDERATION

Should the mother be required to undertake urinalysis testing 48 hours prior to spending time with the child at the maternal grandmother’s residence on Christmas day in 2025 and on the child’s birthday in 2026 per the father’s proposal at proposed orders 8, and 9 and 11(c)

While this judgment will be delivered after the dates specified in the proposed orders, having heard arguments on this issue, I have considered the matter and, ultimately, do not impose upon the mother a requirement that she submit to urinalysis testing as proposed by the father at orders 8, 9 and 11(c) of the minute of order for the following reasons.
Firstly, the father’s proposal must be viewed in the context of a suite of protective orders, made largely by consent between the parties, which impose a rigorous testing regime upon the mother and additional safeguards against the potential risk of the mother experiencing a relapse into illicit drug use. By way of summary, these safeguards include:
(1) The requirement that all time between the child and the mother remain supervised – firstly at a supervised contact centre, and later under the supervision of the maternal grandmother or her nominee – until such time as strict testing conditions are successfully met.

(2) The mother must undergo a chain of custody hair testing for illicit drugs every three months, commencing February 2026, on at least four occasions.

(3) The testing must comply with stringent international standards (ISO/IEC 17024:2005) and Society of Hair Testing (SoHT) guidelines.

(4) After completing the initial testing regime, the mother will be subject to further testing within seven days of any request by the father or maternal grandmother for an additional 12-month period.

(5) The mother’s time with the child only graduates from supervised to unsupervised time following her provision of four consecutive clean hair follicle tests.

(6) Positive test results, or non-compliance with the testing orders, will extend the requirement that time between the mother and child be supervised; thereby providing for indefinite supervision should the risks posed by the mother’s historical drug use persist.

(7) The mother is restrained from staying overnight at the maternal grandmother’s home until she has produced four consecutive clean hair tests and completed the additional 12-month testing period.

(8) All parties are restrained from exposing the child to anyone under the influence of illicit substances, including the mother.

(9) The maternal grandmother must immediately remove the child from the mother’s presence and notify the father if she suspects the mother is using illicit substances.

Secondly, these safeguards will be implemented in circumstances where the maternal grandmother has a history of acting proactively to shield the child from any exposure to the effects of the mother’s substance addiction – even when such action was contrary to the wishes of the mother. Examples of the maternal grandmother’s pattern of protective conduct is detailed in her affidavit filed 4 November 2025 as follows:
(1) In October 2017 – Upon suspecting the mother had recommenced drug use, the maternal grandmother discussed the matter with the maternal step-grandfather and subsequently agreed to limit the mother’s unsupervised time with the child to ensure her safety [paragraph 48].

(2) In March 2018 – Upon discovering illicit substance use by the mother, the maternal grandmother immediately removed the child from the mother’s care and contacted police [paragraph 50].

(3) In April 2018 – The maternal grandmother reported concerns about the mother’s drug use to Family and Community Services (“FACS”) [paragraph 57].

(4) In April 2018 – The maternal grandmother threatened to contact the Department of Communities and Justice and police when the mother failed to return the child, and followed through with such threats by reporting the child’s removal [paragraph 58–69].

(5) On 2 May 2018 – The maternal grandmother commenced urgent recovery proceedings in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia for the child’s return to her care, despite the mother’s objections [paragraph 62].

(6) From August 2018 onwards – The maternal grandmother refused the mother’s repeated requests to move back into the family home until her completion of a rehabilitation programme [paragraphs 88 and 94].

(7) Throughout 2018 – the maternal grandmother supervised the mother’s time with the child and intervened when behaviour was inappropriate, including asking the mother to leave during visits when she presented as agitated or distracted [paragraph 73].

(8) In December 2019 – the maternal grandmother paid for the mother’s counselling and offered to fund family therapy to reduce conflict and protect the child’s emotional wellbeing, despite hostility from the father [paragraph 125].

(9) From July 2024 – After police confirmed the mother was on bail for drug-related charges, the maternal grandmother implemented protective measures to keep the child safe. The maternal grandmother and the maternal step-grandfather were clear that the mother was no longer able to reside in the house and restricted the child from leaving the house with the mother, thus prioritising the child’s safety over the mother’s wishes [paragraphs 403–419].

Furthermore, at paragraph [70] of the Family Report dated 18 June 2025, the report writer acknowledges that the maternal grandmother “has been a stable and protective factor throughout [the child’s] life, proactively mitigating risk whilst balancing normalcy.”
Thirdly, the additional urinalysis tests proposed by the father would be largely tokenistic in so far as, if mother were to have unfortunately resumed drug use, she could partake in the consumption of drugs after she underwent the urinalysis test without such consumption being detected. This would render the orders ineffective at achieving their stated objective.
In other words, the focus should be on the mother’s presentation. The maternal grandmother has demonstrated capacity to detect when the mother is behaving in a dysregulated manner and remove the child from the mother’s presence to prevent their exposure to any inappropriate conduct that that might be occurring or likely to occur. The evidence satisfies me that the maternal grandmother is likely to continue to take a similarly responsible approach in the future.
I am therefore satisfied that there is no need to include the additional words proposed by the father in respect to paragraphs 8, 9 and 11(c). This is in circumstances where I am satisfied that, even without the additional testing requirements, it will be safe for the child to spend time with the mother in terms of s 60CC(2)(a) of the Act due to the above listed safeguards, and that it will be in the child’s best interests to spend time with the mother in terms of s 60CC(2)(e) of the Act – which requires the Court to consider the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so.

What should be the arrangements for the child’s graduated increase in time with the father?

In circumstances where proposed order 17(a) specifically relates to time between the father and child on 7, 14 and 21 December 2025, and noting that such dates have since passed and do not form part of a repeated time arrangement, I have declined to make any orders in respect to this issue.
At proposed order 17(b), the maternal grandmother proposes that the child’s time with the father commence at 11:00am on every third Saturday from 3 January 2026 until 5.00pm on Sunday rather than commencing at 10:00am, as proposed by the father. This was in circumstances where the maternal grandmother, through her Counsel, advised the Court that the child undergoes tutoring of a Saturday morning.
The difficulty I have in considering this issue is that I infer that it is beneficial for the child to have supplementary assistance with her education but, other than a brief reference in the Family Report at paragraph 90 in support of tutoring generally, there is an absence of evidence before the Court as to the nature of the tutoring and, specifically, why the tutoring cannot be scheduled at a time which does not conflict with the father’s time with the child.
In the absence of that evidence, I must be guided by the best interests considerations set out in s 60CC of the Act. In particular, I have had regard to the benefit of the child being able to have a relationship with her parents, including, relevantly, the father (s 60CC(2)(e)). In circumstances where the maternal grandmother’s opposition to the child commencing to spend time with the father at 10:00 am was not based on an issue of risk, the balance, in my view, falls in favour of the father particularly, in circumstances where the child’s time with the father will be relatively limited. Therefore, I do not amend proposed order 17(b) to replace 10:00 am with 11:00am.

Should the child’s time with the father increase to more frequent and longer periods as proposed by the father or be limited to the arrangements proposed by the maternal grandmother?

The maternal grandmother contends that the father’s time should increase from 17 April 2026 so that the child spends every third weekend from after school Friday, or 3.00pm if a non-school day until 5.00pm on Sunday. This is agreed between the parties. However, the father also proposes, at proposed order 17(d), that the time should further increase from Friday 10 July 2026 such that the child spends time with him every third weekend from after school Friday (or 3.00pm if a non-school day) until before school Monday (or 9.00am if a non-school day). While the maternal grandmother agrees that time should also increase from that date, she proposes that such time occur from after school Thursday (or 3.00pm if a non-school day) until 5.00pm Sunday.
The maternal grandmother, through her Counsel, advanced this proposal on the basis that, following time spent with the father, the child is said to return to the maternal grandmother’s care in a heightened emotional state. The difficulty I have with that submission is the absence of any evidence identifying the nature or extent of the child’s emotional presentation upon her return. It is readily conceivable, however, that where a child has experienced an active and enjoyable weekend, she may present with an elevated mood and, potentially, increased tiredness.
Balanced against that possibility, I find considerable merit in the submission of the Independent Children’s Lawyer that, given the tension that exists between the maternal grandmother and the father, it would be preferable to limit the number of face-to-face changeovers to which the child is exposed. That objective is best achieved if changeovers occur at the child’s school, with the father dropping the child at school on Monday morning and the child being collected from school on Monday afternoon.
While there has been no suggestion that the parties have engaged in aggressive conduct towards one another at changeovers, all parties acknowledge an element of tension exists. It is in the child’s emotional best interests for the child to be protected from that tension to the greatest extent possible. On that basis, I prefer the orders proposed by the father and will make order 17(d) as proposed by the father.
In addition to this, the father proposes a further staged increase in time commencing from 1 October 2026 such that the child spends time with him every third weekend from after school Thursday (or 3pm if a non-school day) until before school Monday (or 9am if a non-school day). The maternal grandmother does not agree with this further increase in time.
Again, I have considered this issue in terms of the guidance provided in s 60CC(2)(e) of the Act. In so doing, I note that, at paragraph 118 of the Family Report by the Court Child Expert, Ms N, dated 18 June 2025, it was recommended that the child “gradually increase to spending each alternate weekend with her father, along with each alternate Wednesday afternoon.”
The time proposed by the father is, in effect, less than that which was recommended by the Court Child Expert. In circumstances where the father will have relatively limited time with the child – and where it was observed that both the child and the father “delighted in one another and reciprocated affection”— I consider it appropriate to include the two further incremental increases in time as proposed by the father in paragraph 17(e) of the proposed orders (Family Report dated 18 June 2025).

Should the child spend additional time with the father during school holidays?

The maternal grandmother contends that there should be no increase in the amount of time that the child spends with the father during school holidays, as is set out in the father’s proposed order 18.
The Family Report dated 18 June 2025 does not contain a specific recommendation in respect to the child spending additional time with the father during school holidays. However, at paragraph 17, the report records that final orders made on 22 August 2022 provided for the child’s time with her father to increase to five nights per fortnight and half of the school holidays by 31 January 2024. It is important to note that those arrangements were contemplated in circumstances where the child was to spend time with both her father and the maternal grandmother.
Accordingly, the Court is without relevant expert assistance as to whether the child should spend additional time with the father during school holidays, save to the extent that the Court Child Expert, in her report dated 18 June 2025, recommended a regime under which the child would spend a significantly greater amount of time with the father than has ultimately been agreed to by the parties.
Again, having regard to the provisions of s 60CC(2)(e) of the Act, and in circumstances where the child will otherwise spend a relatively limited amount of time with her father, I consider it to be in the child’s best interests to have additional block time with the father during school holidays. I therefore accept the father’s proposal that the child’s time during school holiday periods increase from four nights to five nights, as set out in proposed order 18.

What should be the arrangements for the child to spend time with the father on Father’s Day

The maternal grandmother proposes that the child should spend time with the father to mark the occasion of Father’s Day only on the day itself; specifically, from 10:00 am until 5:00 pm. By contrast, the father proposes that the child spend time with him from 5:00 pm on the Saturday before Father’s Day until before school on the following Monday.
Once again, in circumstances where the child spends a relatively limited amount of time with the father, and in the absence of a reference to risks to the child, I am satisfied that, consistent with s 60CC(2)(e) of the Act, it is in the child’s best interests to spend additional time with the father. Further, and having regard to the concerns I have earlier expressed in relation to changeovers, the father’s proposal—which would result in the changeover occurring at the child’s school rather than by way of a face‑to‑face interaction between the father and the maternal grandmother—is, for the reasons already given, preferable.
Accordingly, I will make order 19(b) as proposed by the father.

What should be the amount of time that the child spends with her father on Christmas Day?

The parties agree that the child should spend time with the father on Christmas Day –such that in the alternate years, the child will spend the night of Christmas Eve and the morning of Christmas Day with the father. However, the precise timing of this arrangement remains in dispute between the father and the maternal grandmother. The father proposes, at proposed order 19(c) and 19(d), that the child should spend time with him, in one alternate year, from 3.00pm on 25 December until 5.00 pm on 26 December. The maternal grandmother proposes that such time occur from 11.00 am on 25 December until 11.00 am on 26 December. In the other alternate year, the father proposes that the child spend time with him from 1.00 pm on 24 December until 3.00 pm on 25 December 2025. The maternal grandmother proposes that this time occur from 5.00 pm on 24 December until 11.00 am on 25 December. Neither party made submissions as to why their respective proposals should be preferred over those advanced by the other.
This presents a difficulty for the Court. On balance, in circumstances where the child’s primary residence is with the maternal grandmother, I will make orders that suit the arrangements of that household.
I therefore make order 19(c) and 19(d) as proposed by the maternal grandmother.
The father and maternal grandmother were also in dispute as to whether the child would spend the night of Christmas in 2025, and each alternate year thereafter, with the father or the grandmother. As the 2025 Christmas period has lapsed, I have made orders for the arrangements to commence in 2026 and 2027 respectively and continue each alternate year thereafter.

What should be the arrangements for the child to spend time with the maternal grandmother, her mother and her father on other special occasions.

The parties agree that the child should spend time on her birthday with the maternal grandmother and each of her parents. The conflict arises insofar as the parties cannot agree on when such time shall occur.
Once again, there is limited evidence before me to enable me to properly consider this issue. However, there was no challenge to the submission by Counsel for the maternal grandmother that, when the child is in her care, it is customary for the maternal grandmother to take the child to the zoo on the morning of her birthday. Both the maternal grandmother and the mother indicated that there would be no difficulty with the mother attending that zoo visit together with the maternal grandmother and the child, thereby enabling the child’s time with her mother on her birthday to coincide with time spent with the maternal grandmother.
Relevantly, in the context of s 60CC(2)(f) –which requires the Court to consider anything else relevant to the particular circumstances of the child – I am satisfied that such a visit would be an enriching experience for the child and one that she would both enjoy and look forward to. Accordingly, the orders I make will provide for the mother to spend time with the child from 10:00 am until 2:00 pm on the child’s birthday 2026 and from 2:00 pm until 6:00 pm on the same day with her father.
Once again, there is an absence of evidence that would enable the Court to definitively adjudicate between the parties’ competing contentions as to whether the time the child spends with the father on her birthday should incrementally increase as she grows older, as proposed by the father in orders 19(e) and 19(f).
Nevertheless, having regard to s 60CC(2)(e) of the Act, I am satisfied that, in the absence of any identified safety concerns, it is in the child’s best interests to spend additional time with the father on her birthday consistent with her developing maturity. While this may result in the child not attending the zoo with the maternal grandmother and her mother on the morning of her birthday in some years, those arrangements could be adjusted to accommodate the schedule proposed by the father.
In terms of s 60CC(2)(f) of the Act, I find that it would be enjoyable for the child to, every second year, receive birthday gifts from the father first thing in the morning on her birthday. I will therefore make orders 19(e) and (f) as proposed by the father.

What should be the arrangements for the child to spend time with the father on culturally significant days?

The maternal grandmother agrees that the child should spend time with the father during NAIDOC Week in the manner proposed by the father at proposed order 19(h).
The maternal grandmother is also agreeable to the child spending time with the father on 26 May each year for the purposes of taking the child to a Sorry Day event. She proposes, however, that such time be from 10:00 am until 5:00 pm and subject to the father “not removing [X] from school for the purpose of this event unless there is a sanctioned and/or organised school event that is taking place away from the school grounds.”
Once again, there is an absence of evidence before the Court that would materially assist in adjudicating this issue. The competing considerations essentially boiled down to the desirability of minimising disruption to the child’s schooling on the one hand, and the cultural significance to the child of attending a Sorry Day event on the other.
In considering this matter, I am required to have regard to the provisions of s 60CC(3) to which I have earlier referred. On balance, and given the child’s age, I am not satisfied there she would suffer any significant detriment from missing one day of school, or part of one day. In any event, any such detriment would need to be weighed against the benefit of the child engaging in a culturally significant event in the context of her Aboriginal heritage.
On balance, I prefer the orders proposed by the father and will make order 19(i) in those terms.

What should be the amount of time that the child spends with the father on 26 January.

In considering this issue, I note that 26 January has particular significance in terms of the child’s Anglo-Celtic culture and Aboriginal culture, respectively.
Therefore, it is, in my opinion, desirable that the child spends a reasonable period of time with both the maternal grandmother and the father on 26 January each year. In that context, the maternal grandmother, through her Counsel, advised the Court that it is customary for the maternal grandmother to invite friends and extended relatives to her home on 26 January for breakfast. Having regard to the provisions of ss 60CC(2)(f) and 60CC(3) of the Act, I am satisfied that attending such a gathering would likely be enjoyable for the child and provide her with an opportunity to strengthen a bond with her extended maternal family and friends.
At the same time, the times proposed by the maternal grandmother at proposed order 19(j) provide for the child to spend a significant amount of time with her father on 26 January.
For these reasons, I prefer the orders proposed by the maternal grandmother and will make order 19 (j) in those terms.

Should the mother be restrained from residing at the home of the maternal grandmother until the child is 14 years of age.

As explained by Kirby J in AMS v AIF (1999) 199 CLR 160 at [193], the Family law Act 1975 (Cth) “is enacted to take effect within a society of a particular character whose members enjoy a high measure of personal freedom, diminished only to the extent that the law obliges.”
Citing that decision, the Full Court in Wagstaff & Wagstaff (2022) FLC 94–098 (“Wagstaff”) stated at [2] that “orders requiring a parent to live in a particular place should be made only in rare or exceptional cases”. Expanding upon this principle, it is my view that the same reasoning must necessarily apply to orders directing where a person should not live.
Applying that reasoning, with which I agree, the father has failed to establish that this case falls within the exceptional category referred to in Wagstaff. Further, the order as proposed by the father is unsatisfactorily vague insofar as it makes no meaningful distinction between the mother staying at the maternal grandmother’s home and “residing” there.
For these reasons, I will not make order 28 as proposed by the father.

Should the father be permitted to change the Child’s name?

In support of this proposed order, the father notes that the Order 2 of Consent Orders made on 22 August 2022 provided authority for the father to “apply to the Registrar Births, Deaths and Marriages for the child registered as [X Ila] born in 2017 to now be registered as [X Ila-Irving]”. Critically, Order 2 of the new Consent Orders made on this day have the effect of discharging all prior parenting orders; including the above referenced order which previously provided authority for the change of name to occur.
The difficulty I have with the father’s proposed order, is that the father did not seek such an order in his Response to the Initiating Application nor in his Case Outline. Additionally, the father has failed to explain why he has not acted upon the authorisation provided to him on 22 August 2022.
The indolence in the father taking steps in accordance with that authorisation is not insignificant. The issue of change of the child’s name has not been considered by the Court Child Experts who have provided a report in this matter.
Further, as the issue was not raised until the eleventh hour of the proceedings, there is no information before the Court as to the child’s views in respect of having her surname changed. This is significant, it precludes the Court from having regard to s 60CC(2)(b) of the Act, which requires the court to consider to “any views expressed by the child.”
The child is now almost nine years of age and cannot reasonably be expected to have no views as to whether she wishes her name to be changed from that which she has used and been known for the entirety of her life.
On that basis, and in the absence of my ability to consider the wishes of the child, I do not make orders 30 and 31 as proposed by the father.

Should the mother be restrained from attending school events until such time as she has provided the maternal grandmother and the father with two consecutive hair follicle tests which are clear of illicit substances

For similar reasons to those which I have previously set out in respect to whether the mother should undertake uranalysis testing prior to attending the paternal grandmother’s home for Christmas and the child’s birthday, I will not make order 35 as proposed by the father.
In circumstances where the orders put in place a rigorous drug testing regime, that has been agreed to by the mother, and where the evidence satisfies me that the maternal grandmother has consistently acted protectively of the child, including in circumstances where doing so has been contrary to the wishes of the mother, I am not satisfied that there is a need for the additional order sought by the father.
This is particularly so in circumstances where the mother’s attendance at any such school sporting or extracurricular event will occur in a context where the maternal grandmother will also be present. Based on the maternal grandmother’s past conduct, I am satisfied that she would act protectively of the child should the mother, contrary to expectations, relapse into the use of illicit substances.
Accordingly, I will not include the additional words proposed by the father in order 35, and instead make orders in the terms orally agreed between the mother, grandmother and Independent Children’s Lawyer at the Final Hearing, which require the grandmother’s attendance at any school event for the period which, under these orders, the mother’s time with the child is to be supervised.

What arrangements should be in place in respect to overseas travel?

This issue arises in circumstances where the father has several relatives who reside in New Zealand and where some members of the extended maternal family reside in the United States of America.
To their credit, the parties agree that the child should be permitted to undertake international travel with the father. There is, however, disagreement as to whether such travel is deferred until 2027 or 2028 and whether such time should be restricted to a period of ten days or 21 days. In my view, these issues are inter-related.
In considering this question, I have had regard to the Family Report dated 18 July 2025. While the issue of travel is not specifically addressed, the report writer observed that the child is “positively responding” to a more stable environment and emphasised the importance of her “settling into a more predictable routine, which will be critical, to foster improvements in establishing her sense of security and support her continued development” (Family Report dated 18 July 2025, paragraph 91).
In circumstances where the child will not previously have spent a period of 21 days away from the maternal grandmother, and where overnight time with the father will have increased only to five nights during school holidays by 2026, I consider it appropriate that international travel be deferred until 2028 and that its duration be limited to ten days. I therefore make orders in the terms proposed by the maternal grandmother.

Should make up time be provided to the maternal grandmother in the event of the father taking the child overseas

The maternal grandmother proposed that order 43(d) be amended so to require the father, in the event that he takes the child on an overseas holiday, to provide make‑up time not only to the mother but also to the maternal grandmother.
The father agrees that make up time should be provided to the mother. That concession is appropriate in circumstances where the child’s time with the mother is relatively limited.
The more relevant question is whether make up time should similarly be provided to the maternal grandmother.
In circumstances where, for reasons that I have earlier explained, the orders that I make will restrict overseas travel time to a period of 10 days, the time that the child will be absent from the care of the maternal grandmother will be limited. Further, where the child’s time with the father is, as I have earlier explained, itself relatively limited, there is, in those circumstances, no need to provide compensatory make up time to the maternal grandmother.

ORDERS

Accordingly, for all these reasons, I will the make the orders as proposed by the parties with the associated amendments referred to in the body of this decision.
